context.description: A philosophical nonfiction passage argues that awareness is voluntarily self-limited and explains "dual awareness" as simultaneously noticing the outer world and one’s inner responses. It frames this as a common but often forgotten human experience.
normalization_notes: Derived from continuous German nonfiction prose on consciousness and awareness.

rows.paragraph_text: Awareness is described as voluntarily constrained, like a bandwidth limit, because expanding it would disrupt inner coherence. The text explains dual awareness as simultaneous attention to the outer world and to one’s inner reactions, perceptions, and feelings.

The passage argues that people can unite outward and inward observation in a universal awareness, even if they do not recognize the experience. It presents such moments as common, temporary, and easily forgotten.

content.free_text: nonfiction_book_page book_body_content theoretical_discussion Bewusstseinsreisen - Version 2, page 46, German nonfiction prose on awareness, self-limitation, and dual awareness of inner and outer worlds.
